petitioner_background: The petitioner is a luthier (a maker and repairer of stringed instruments) seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. Based on references to Italian-language documents, competitions in Italy, and Italian violin-making schools, the petitioner appears to be of Italian background. He had previously been approved for at least one O-1 nonimmigrant visa petition and is currently employed by a U.S. company (referenced through letters from its President) that has been operating for over 40 years, where he repairs and restores stringed instruments and makes new instruments.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, the petitioner identified three prizes/awards: (1) the 'Category YOUNGSTERS 1st AWARD Section VIOLA' (characterized by petitioner as '1st Award, Section Viola'), submitted in foreign language with a certified English translation on appeal; (2) first prize in the '2nd National Lutherie Contest YOUNG LUTHIERS,' a contest reserved for pupils of a specific school, supported by an article from a website titled 'Violin making contest in [location]' explaining the competition had three sections (professional violin makers, open contest, and a contest reserved to juniors or graduates holding a diploma for not longer than two years), plus a similar article and an article titled 'Lutherie. National Contest' the petitioner asserted appeared in a publication (though the publication name did not appear on the document or translation); and (3) second prize at the '4th NATIONAL CONTEST OF LUTHERIE,' for which the petitioner submitted only a Google Translate rendering of what appeared to be the competition's rules, without the foreign language original.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the petitioner failed to meet this criterion. For the Youngsters 1st Award Section Viola, the petitioner provided no documentary evidence demonstrating the award is recognized beyond the presenting organization. For the Young Luthiers contest award, the supporting articles did not identify the award beyond describing the contest sections, and the article did not suggest that the junior competitions (limited to pupils of the school) are nationally or internationally recognized; additionally, the petitioner did not provide information about the publication in which the article appeared, and one article had no publication name on either the foreign language document or translation. The article about the national contest indicated an international jury of luthier masters but did not demonstrate that the youth portion of the competition was nationally or internationally recognized. For the 4th National Contest of Lutherie award, the Google Translate document had no probative value because the foreign language original was not provided; regardless, the translation did not address selection criteria for specific awards beyond fees and experience, did not establish the selection criteria for the award the petitioner won, and did not demonstrate the award is nationally or internationally recognized.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Within the initial filing, the petitioner provided several forms of evidence under this criterion, but each was in a foreign language and was not accompanied by a certified translation. The petitioner did not submit additional evidence about him relating to his work in the field in his RFE response. On appeal, the petitioner provided an article from a publication with a certified translation of the story.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not meet this criterion. The initial evidence failed because it was in a foreign language without certified translations complying with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The article submitted on appeal had multiple deficiencies: the certified translation did not include the portion bearing the petitioner's name; the article was about a competition rather than about the petitioner relating to his work in the field (citing Negro-Plumpe v. Okin, which upheld a finding that articles about a show are not about the actor); and the translation did not include the publication's date nor any reference to the article's author as required by the regulation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner did not assert eligibility under this criterion throughout the proceedings before the director, though the director discussed the criterion in his decision. On appeal, the petitioner did not contest the director's findings or offer additional arguments or evidence under this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner had abandoned any assertions under this criterion by failing to contest the director's findings or offer additional arguments on appeal, citing Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen. and Hristov v. Roark. Accordingly, the petitioner had not submitted qualifying evidence under this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Initially and in response to the RFE, the petitioner did not address this criterion. On appeal, the petitioner asserted he performed in a critical role for his current employer and provided three letters from the President of his employer company dated May 12, 2011, March 12, 2013, and July 17, 2014. The letters indicated the petitioner is highly gifted, that his abilities and technical skills in making new instruments are extraordinary, that the President is fortunate to have the petitioner working for his company, that the petitioner brought a vast portfolio of experience, and that the petitioner is one of the people the company can trust to repair and restore instruments. Evidence also indicated the business has been operating for over 40 years.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the evidence sufficiently demonstrated that the petitioner's employer enjoys a distinguished reputation, but concluded the petitioner did not meet the criterion. The President did not assert that the petitioner performs in a leading role as demonstrated through his position within the overall organizational hierarchy. Moreover, the President explained the business had been operating for over 40 years but did not describe what impact the petitioner's work had on the company such that his role had been critical for the business. The AAO also noted that while the President asserted there is a shortage of sufficiently trained and experienced luthiers to work on valuable stringed instruments, the issue of whether similarly-trained workers are available in the United States falls under Department of Labor jurisdiction, citing Matter of New York State Dep't of Transp.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, agreeing with the Director that the petitioner failed to submit the requisite initial evidence satisfying at least three of the ten regulatory criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), and had not submitted evidence of a one-time achievement. The petitioner therefore failed to satisfy the antecedent regulatory requirement under Kazarian, making a final merits determination unnecessary. Although the AAO noted it need not provide a final merits determination, it indicated that a review of the evidence in the aggregate supports a finding that the petitioner has not demonstrated the level of expertise required for the classification sought. The AAO also rejected the argument that prior O-1 nonimmigrant approval compelled approval, noting that the O-1 arts standard of 'distinction' is dramatically lower than the immigrant 'extraordinary ability' standard, and that the petitioner's education was not explained as relevant to the higher classification sought.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F.Supp.2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011), aff'd, 683 F.3d 1030 (9th Cir. 2012)
Q Data Consulting, Inc. v. INS, 293 F. Supp. 2d 25 (D.D.C. 2003)
IKEA US v. US Dept. of Justice, 48 F. Supp. 2d 22 (D.D.C. 1999)
Fedin Brothers Co. Ltd. v. Sava, 724 F. Supp. 1103 (E.D.N.Y. 1989)
Texas A&M Univ. v. Upchurch, 99 Fed. Appx. 556, 2004 WL 1240482 (5th Cir. 2004)
Matter of Church Scientology International, 19 I&N Dec. 593 (Comm'r 1988)
Sussex Engg. Ltd. v. Montgomery, 825 F.2d 1084 (6th Cir. 1987), cert. denied, 485 U.S. 1008 (1988)
Glara Fashion, Inc. v. Holder, 11 CIV. 889 PAE, 2012 WL 352309 (S.D.N.Y. Feb. 3, 2012)
Royal Siam v. Chertoff, 484 F.3d 139 (1st Cir. 2007)
Tapis Int'l v. INS, 94 F.Supp.2d 172 (D. Mass. 2000)
Louisiana Philharmonic Orchestra v. INS, 44 F.Supp.2d 800 (E.D. La. 1999), aff'd, 248 F.3d 1139 (5th Cir. 2001), cert. denied, 534 U.S. 819 (2001)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Matter of New York State Dep't of Transp., 22 I&N Dec. 215 (Assoc. Comm'r 1998)
Soltane v. United States Dep't of Justice, 381 F.3d 143 (3d Cir. 2004)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Several procedural issues were noted but none involved fraud. First, translations of foreign language letters submitted with the initial petition filing failed to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) because the translator(s) did not certify competency to translate or that the translations were accurate and complete, rendering them without probative value; however, the AAO reviewed them anyway because the director's RFE did not specify which translations were noncompliant. Second, for the 4th National Contest of Lutherie award, the petitioner submitted only a Google Translate rendering of the competition rules without the foreign language original, which the AAO found had no probative value. Third, the article submitted on appeal under the published material criterion lacked publication date and author information as required by regulation, and the translation omitted the portion bearing the petitioner's name. Fourth, the petitioner was found to have abandoned the original contributions criterion by failing to contest the director's findings on appeal.
